source_detail: agent: economy lane — run-mechanics build (self-noted follow-up)
title: Replace run-energy weight heuristic with real per-item weight
raw: (seed: session follow-up) run.js — swap the weight heuristic for a sum of real item.weight when the economy lane adds the field.
interpreted: Run-energy drain in src/engine/run.js uses a placeholder encumbrance model (flat per-equip-slot weights + 0.5kg per non-stackable inventory item). Add a real numeric `weight` (kg) to the item database and make playerWeight() sum it, so drain reflects actual gear. Naturally splits: (a) DATA — add `weight` to items.json rows; (b) CODE — read/sum it in run.js and delete the heuristic.

signals: playerWeight() derived from summed item weights, not the heuristic
a heavy set drains run energy faster than a light set by the expected ratio
acceptance: items.json rows carry a numeric weight
run.js playerWeight() sums real weights and the heuristic is removed

guardrails: no build tooling
engine edit is not overnight-safe; the data half CAN be split out as overnight-safe
notes: SPLIT SUGGESTED: file the data half (add `weight` to items.json by category/tier) as a separate overnight-safe GH item; keep the run.js wiring needs-strong-model.

source_detail: agent: world-gen lane — spawn visual overhaul (observed 36fps in keep view)
title: Trim wall-brick terrain detail to recover keep-view framerate
raw: (seed: session follow-up) Perf: ~60fps outdoors but ~36fps in a full-screen-of-brick-walls keep view — tunable; could drop the two brick-joint rects.
interpreted: drawTerrain() in src/main.js draws ~6 fillRects per WALL tile. Views dominated by walls (keep interior) drop to ~36fps. Reduce per-wall draw ops (drop the two staggered brick-joint rects, and/or gate fine detail by zoom / visible-tile count) to hold >=55fps without losing the capped-stone read.

signals: fps in a full-screen keep-wall view >= 55 (topbar fps readout)
walls still read as capped stone brick after the trim

guardrails: main.js render code — NOT overnight-safe
do not touch scene/input/camera
notes: Hot path is detailWall() inside drawTerrain().

source_detail: agent: economy lane — Alchemy skill build
title: Add more discoverable Alchemy tonic recipes
raw: (seed: session follow-up) Alchemy MVP ships 3 tonics (Stamina/Restore/Antidote); add more starter recipes / richer tonics.
interpreted: Extend the Alchemy recipe set with 2-4 more discoverable tonics (e.g., a weight-reducing 'featherfoot' draught, skilling-boost tonics) from existing foraged reagents + cross-skill ingredients. Data-shaped: add RECIPES entries + item defs; reuse existing effect hooks (run energy / HP / poison) — no new systems.

signals: new recipes discoverable via the cauldron
each new tonic has a working effect + description string

guardrails: keep effects within existing hooks unless owner approves a new effect type (temp skill boosts need a decay system = not overnight-safe)
no emoji in UI chrome
notes: Recipes live in alchemy.js RECIPES today; could migrate to a data file.

title: Click-to-pick-up doesn't auto-walk to the ground item
raw: Picking up items off the ground seems to be a little bit broken — they don't seem to be a thing that I can just click and then automatically walk to and pick up. It seems like I need to walk to the tile in order to pick it up, but it should just automatically walk to the tile when I click to pick it up.
interpreted: EXPECTED: click a ground item from a distance → the avatar auto-walks onto that tile and picks it up. ACTUAL: nothing auto-walks; you must manually walk onto the tile before it's collected. The client interaction wiring already exists and reads correct (onPointerDown ~L799 `if (ground.length) return startPickup(tx,ty)`; startPickup ~L898 sets pickupTarget + findPath ONTO the tile; gameTick ~L1095 re-paths, ~L1126 picks up when tileX/tileY == target) — so the break is almost certainly the multiplayer server-authoritative MOVEMENT seam, not the pickup logic. Audit to confirm which: (H1) with server-auth movement only walkTo/travel intents are sent to the server; the pickupTarget auto-walk path never becomes a server move intent, so the avatar doesn't move and pickup only fires once you walk there manually. (H2) Game.groundItems is server-synced and the client's local list is empty/stale at click time, so `ground.length===0` and onPointerDown falls through to `walkTo(tx,ty)` (walks NEAR, never picks up).

signals: with the Node worldserver running: drop an item, walk a few tiles away, click it → avatar walks onto the tile and auto-collects
right-click → Take behaves the same
acceptance: click a ground item at distance → auto-walk + pickup (verified in-server)
no regression to walk / interact / attack click-routing

guardrails: main.js input/interaction = needs a strong model, NOT overnight-safe
if the true fix is a server-side move intent it becomes needs-owner — do NOT touch server/** or save.js
never edit client code to bypass the landing page
notes: AUDIT FIRST (H1 vs H2). While in here, check whether click-to-INTERACT (nodes/altar/transport) shares the same missing-move-intent symptom under server-auth movement. Core-loop UX bug → P1.

title: Remote players lag then snap to position and clip through walls
raw: There seems to be a lag when I see somebody, they walk to where they really are and that sometimes shows them going through walls.
interpreted: Other players update in coarse jumps then rubber-band/catch-up to their true position (visible lag), and the smoothing between updates interpolates in straight lines that pass THROUGH walls. Likely cause: remote positions arrive on the ~2000ms authoritative server heartbeat (server log: 'authoritative loop @ 2000ms'), so each update is a big jump the client lerps naively (no path/collision awareness). Want: smooth, collision-aware interpolation of remote avatars between updates (interpolate along a walkable path, and/or raise remote-position cadence) so others glide tile-to-tile and never cross impassable terrain.

signals: in a 2-client test a moving remote player glides tile-to-tile with no snap/rubber-band
the remote avatar never renders on/through a wall tile
acceptance: remote players interpolate smoothly between updates
remote path never crosses non-walkable tiles
smoke green; verified with two clients on the Node server

guardrails: client interpolation/render — NOT overnight-safe
raising the server heartbeat touches server/index.mjs = needs-owner; prefer a client-side interpolation fix first
never bypass the landing page
notes: RESEARCH first: is the win a client-side interpolation fix (cheap, client-only) or a higher server update rate (server change, bandwidth)? Same MP-fidelity cluster as GH-0006.

title: Players don't see the same monsters (mobs aren't shared)
raw: They also don't appear to be seeing the same monsters, so they can't see each other killing things.
interpreted: Each client renders its OWN local monster set, so two players in the same area see different mobs and cannot witness each other fighting/killing — breaking the shared-world promise. Want: server-authoritative, SHARED monsters — the server owns spawns/positions/HP/death for a region and broadcasts them to every client there; clients render server mobs instead of locally-spawned ones. This is the 'Phase 4 server-authoritative' work sketched in docs/MULTIPLAYER_ARCHITECTURE.md (ROADMAP notes only 'Stage-1' mobs shipped).
files_likely: src/main.js (render server-owned mobs; stop local spawns where shared)

signals: two clients in the same region see the SAME monsters at the same positions/HP
when one player kills a mob the other sees it die and it stays dead for both
acceptance: shared authoritative mobs for at least one region, verified across two clients
no desync of spawn/position/HP/death between clients

guardrails: server-authoritative mobs = Phase 4, explicitly on the CRITICAL_PATH 'NOT for smaller models' list
touches server/index.mjs + architecture + possibly world-state — OWNER / strong-model only
use api() in net/config.js; never hardcode server URLs
notes: HEADLINE 'truly two-player' gap. Start from docs/MULTIPLAYER_ARCHITECTURE.md Phase 4; scope as incremental shippable steps (one region first). Large — L effort, multiple commits. Related to GH-0005.

title: Right-click a monster to view its drop table
raw: It would be nice to be able to right click a monster and pull up their drop table.
interpreted: Add a right-click context-menu option on a monster (e.g. 'View drops') that opens a view listing that monster's drop table — items + rarity/quantity — sourced from src/data/drop_tables.json + monsters.json (the same tables systems/drops.js rolls). Two parts: (a) a small hook in main.js rightClickMenu (~L833) adding the option for npc/guard targets; (b) a data-driven drop-table view in the side panel / a popup.

signals: right-clicking a monster shows a 'View drops' option
selecting it lists that monster's real drop table with rarities
acceptance: view shows the correct table from data
no emoji in UI chrome (use icons.js / itemIcons.js)

guardrails: the main.js rightClickMenu hook = needs a strong model (input code)
no emoji in UI chrome — use src/ui/icons.js + src/data/itemIcons.js
notes: Nice-to-have per owner. The panel + data-read half is nearly overnight-safe; the main.js input hook is the strong-model part — could split into two GH items.

title: Night tint only covers the game view, not the whole screen
raw: The night mode does not cover the map, so I don't know if that's an issue with how we've implemented it or what. And sorry. Not the map, but the whole screen.
interpreted: At night (world clock = Night) the day/night darkening only tints the game canvas — the side panel, topbar, and chat log stay bright — so 'night' doesn't read across the UI. ROOT CAUSE (found): the `#daylight-overlay` multiply-blend div (main.js ~L530-534, css `position:absolute; inset:0; mix-blend-mode:multiply`) is appended to `#game-panel`, so it only ever spans the game view, not the browser window. DESIGN QUESTION FIRST (owner call): is game-view-only tint intended (keeps UI chrome readable), or should night darken the whole screen? If whole-screen: move/duplicate the overlay to a fixed full-window element (or tint panel+topbar too) while keeping UI text legible under multiply. Secondary: the night tint also looked faint at 20:53 in the screenshot — check whether night intensity is strong enough (daylight curve at main.js ~L2436).
files_likely: index.html (overlay CSS / a full-window night layer)

signals: at Night, the darkening spans the agreed area (whole screen, or the full game view per the decision) — not just part of it
UI text stays readable under the tint
acceptance: night tint coverage matches that intent

guardrails: overlay element is created in main.js create() = needs a strong model; the sizing/CSS could move to index.html
world-gen owns world tinting (worldClock daylight factor) — coordinate if touching the curve
no emoji in UI chrome
notes: Owner unsure if it's a bug or intended — resolve the design question before coding. Small once decided (change overlay parent/position, or add a fixed night layer). Owner playtest, 2026-07-03. PARKED — SUPERSEDED by GH-0010 (owner chose to disable night entirely).

title: Minimap shows many unclear arrows — reduce to ONE tracked-quest objective arrow
raw: We have navigation arrows in the mini map, but for some reason there's many of them, and it's not clear what they're for. There should just be one arrow that directs you to the next objective on your quest when the quest is being tracked, and that's it for arrows.
interpreted: drawMinimap (src/main.js ~L2579) clamps multiple off-view targets to the minimap edge as directional arrows: (1) the tracked-quest marker (drawQuestArrow ~L2621) — which is ALREADY at most one (quests.js questMarkers() returns <=1 marker for the tracked quest only), AND (2) an edge-arrow for every off-view fast-travel TRANSPORT (the carts / mine cart / blood portal added by the travel system, ~L2639). Together those are the 'many arrows' of unclear purpose. DESIRED: exactly ONE minimap arrow, pointing to the tracked quest's next objective, and only when a quest is tracked. Remove the transport directional edge-arrows (and any other non-quest edge arrows); in-view transport ICONS can stay, but no directional arrows for them.

signals: minimap shows at most ONE arrow
that arrow points at the tracked quest's next objective
no arrows when no quest is tracked
off-view transports no longer draw edge arrows
acceptance: <=1 minimap arrow, bound to the tracked quest objective

guardrails: drawMinimap is main.js render — needs a strong model, NOT overnight-safe
the transport edge-arrows came from the fast-travel system (agent-added) — this is the source of the extras
notes: Confirm with owner: keep in-view transport ICONS (just drop their directional arrows), yes? Quest arrow is already singular via questMarkers(); the cleanup is removing the transport (and any player/POI) edge-arrow branch. Owner playtest, 2026-07-03.

title: Disable the night darkening (permanent-day visuals)
raw: Can we disable night, it's not enjoyable.
interpreted: Owner finds the night visuals unenjoyable — remove the day/night DARKENING so the world always reads as daytime. Simplest: neutralize the `#daylight-overlay` (main.js: created ~L530; its colour set from the daylight curve ~L2436 each update) so it stays the clear/noon colour at all times (or hide it) = permanent-day visuals. Keep the WORLD CLOCK ticking (day counter, world events, phase readout) — only the visual dim is removed — unless the owner also wants time frozen. SUPERSEDES GH-0008 (night-tint coverage), now moot.
files_likely: src/main.js (daylight overlay colour update ~2436; overlay create ~530)
src/engine/worldClock.js (daylightFactor — only if freezing time too)

signals: the world never visibly darkens — always daytime lighting at any in-game hour
topbar day/phase readout + world events still work (clock not broken)
acceptance: no night darkening at any time
world clock / events unaffected (unless owner also wants time frozen)

guardrails: overlay lives in main.js render — needs a strong model, but the change is tiny/low-risk (force the day colour or hide the overlay)
world-gen owns worldClock tinting — coordinate if touching daylightFactor
do NOT remove the clock itself (day counter + world events depend on it) unless owner confirms
notes: Owner decision: night 'not enjoyable' → disable. CONFIRM SCOPE: (A) visual-only, keep the clock ticking (recommended) vs (B) also freeze time at day. Supersedes GH-0008.

source_detail: agent: character-render lane — avatar art overhaul, filed at owner's request 2026-07-03
title: Scaffold avatar art-mode loader + PNG manifest + pivot schema
raw: (owner directive 2026-07-03: plug the avatar-art requests into the hopper) The enabler that lets AI-generated PNGs replace the procedural rig piece-by-piece.
interpreted: Add an 'art mode' to src/render/avatar.js so each rig layer (body part, gear overlay, weapon) can be a textured PNG drawn through the EXISTING makeCtx local->world transform (P() + rotation), falling back to the current vector primitive when no art exists. Mirror the item-art pattern already in src/data/itemIcons.js (assets/items/manifest.json + lazy load): add assets/avatar/manifest.json + a pivot/anchor JSON (grip/tip for weapons; shoulder/hip/knee/crown for parts) that externalises the hardcoded local coords (feet y=0, HIP_Y=11, SHOULDER_Y=20, HEAD_Y=26, TORSO_W=8, ARM_LEN=8.5). Ship piece-by-piece; procedural everywhere art hasn't arrived. Approve each part across facings/anims in avatar_preview.html.

signals: a layer with a manifest PNG renders textured art at the correct pivot/rotation in avatar_preview.html
layers without art still draw the procedural primitive (no regression)
no build step added
acceptance: art-mode loader + manifest + pivot schema in place
one part renders from a PNG through P() with correct pivot + swing

guardrails: no build tooling — vanilla ES modules, Phaser from CDN
src/render/avatar.js is render-lane code — needs a strong model
keep the procedural fallback; ship art incrementally
notes: ENABLER for GH-0012..GH-0017. Reuse itemIcons.js hasItemArt/loadItemArtManifest as the manifest+lazy-load template. The pivot JSON is the seam that lets AI parts drop in without editing draw code.

source_detail: agent: character-render lane — avatar art overhaul, filed at owner's request 2026-07-03
title: Avatar art STYLE KIT — locked palette, ref sheet, projection/lighting + pivot schema
raw: (owner directive 2026-07-03) The consistency bible so per-asset AI generation actually matches.
interpreted: Produce the art-direction kit every generated avatar asset conforms to: (1) locked palette swatches — goblin green #6fbf3f + the metal/cloth ramps in src/render/gear.js (bronze #b5793a, iron #8f9196, steel #c2c7ce, mithril, adamant, rune, gold, cloth/leather); (2) a one-paragraph style bible + a reference-sheet image; (3) projection/lighting spec: slightly-top-down 3/4 orthographic, single top-left light, NO baked ground shadow, transparent PNG, authored at 4-8x (~512px parts / 256-512 weapons); (4) the three canonical facings (S front, N back, E side; W mirrors E); (5) the pivot/anchor JSON schema shared with GH-0010. Single best consistency trick: img2img from the current procedural render as a per-part pose scaffold.

signals: a written spec + palette + reference sheet exists that a generator/artist can follow
two independently-generated parts visibly match in palette / light / scale
acceptance: style bible + locked palette + projection/light spec + facing rule + pivot schema documented

guardrails: art direction is an owner-taste call — needs owner sign-off
no emoji in UI chrome
notes: Foundation all art batches (GH-0012..0017) depend on for consistency. I can draft docs/AVATAR_ART_SPEC.md straight from the rig constants on request.

source_detail: owner — 2026-07-03 voice (avatar art initial test run)
title: PILOT — generate 6 high-impact avatar assets for the first manual test
raw: why don't you surface a very high impact asset or half dozen assets that we can do our initial test on in a very limited manual run
interpreted: A minimal, high-wow first batch that both looks great AND stress-tests the whole pipeline end-to-end: a complete FRONT-FACING (S) armed goblin composed of AI-generated parts + one layered overlay + one held/rotating weapon. The six: (1) goblin torso — front; (2) goblin head + separate face layer — front; (3) goblin weapon-arm (upper + forearm/hand) — front; (4) goblin legs (thigh + shin/foot) — front; (5) bronze helm overlay — front (tests layered gear stacking on the head + ear-tip poke-through); (6) one hero weapon held in profile with grip+tip anchors (tests rotation + the attack swing) — suggest the bronze scimitar (what a new player actually holds) or a flashier hero blade for max wow. Composited they form one fully-arted hero goblin and exercise: static parts, a layered overlay, a held+rotating weapon, the locked palette, and pivots. Wire through the GH-0010 loader and approve in avatar_preview.html; N/E facings + the rest of the body follow in GH-0013.

signals: the 6 PNGs render as a single coherent front-facing armed goblin in avatar_preview.html
the weapon rotates/swings on its grip anchor through idle + attack
palette + light match across all 6
acceptance: 6 assets generated to the GH-0011 style kit
composited hero goblin approved by owner in avatar_preview.html
procedural fallback still intact for un-arted layers

guardrails: art generation is a manual/owner pipeline — Forge can't run it
integration path is GH-0010 (needs-strong-model); the 6 images can be generated in parallel
no baked ground shadow; transparent PNG
notes: This IS the owner's requested 'limited manual run' / initial pipeline proof. Depends on the loader (to see them in-game) + style kit (for consistency), but the images can be generated in parallel with GH-0010.
